questions.stem: This schematic shows mobile DNA elements flanking a gene, allowing it to move between plasmid and chromosomal DNA. Which clinically important trait is most commonly carried between bacteria by these elements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Photosynthesis genes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flagellar assembly genes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ribosomal RNA genes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sporulation genes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Antibiotic resistance genes

explanation.summary: Transposons frequently carry drug-resistance genes; when a resistance gene is flanked on both sides by transposon sequences it can be cut out and inserted elsewhere, spreading resistance between plasmids and chromosomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transposons frequently carry drug-resistance genes; when a resistance gene is flanked on both sides by transposon sequences it can be cut out and inserted elsewhere, spreading resistance between plasmids and chromosomes.

questions.stem: In the process shown, a donor bacterium extends a sex pilus to a recipient and transfers plasmid DNA. What is the essential requirement for this form of horizontal gene transfer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exposure to ultraviolet light

choices.label: B
choices.text: Endospore formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Uptake of naked DNA from the environment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct cell-to-cell contact via a pilus/mating bridge

choices.label: E
choices.text: A bacteriophage vector

explanation.summary: Conjugation requires physical contact: the F+ donor forms a sex pilus and mating bridge with the F- recipient and transfers a single strand of the F plasmid, converting the recipient into a donor. No chromosomal DNA and no external vector is involved.
explanation.whyCorrect: Conjugation requires physical contact: the F+ donor forms a sex pilus and mating bridge with the F- recipient and transfers a single strand of the F plasmid, converting the recipient into a donor. No chromosomal DNA and no external vector is involved.

questions.stem: Here the donor's F plasmid has integrated into its chromosome before conjugation with a recipient. Compared with standard F+ conjugation, what is the key consequence of this donor state?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No DNA transfer occurs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flanking chromosomal (donor) DNA is transferred, not just the plasmid

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ribosomes are transferred to the recipient

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the intact free plasmid is transferred

choices.label: E
choices.text: The recipient cell is lysed

explanation.summary: In an Hfr (high-frequency recombination) donor the F plasmid is integrated in the chromosome, so transfer starting at the plasmid origin drags adjacent chromosomal genes into the recipient, allowing recombination of donor chromosomal DNA.
explanation.whyCorrect: In an Hfr (high-frequency recombination) donor the F plasmid is integrated in the chromosome, so transfer starting at the plasmid origin drags adjacent chromosomal genes into the recipient, allowing recombination of donor chromosomal DNA.

questions.stem: This sequence shows a lytic phage that mistakenly packages host DNA and delivers it to a new bacterium. How does this mechanism differ from specialized transduction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Any random fragment of host DNA can be packaged and transferred

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transfer requires a sex pilus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only the phage's own viral DNA is transferred

choices.label: D
choices.text: DNA is taken up from the environment

choices.label: E
choices.text: Only DNA adjacent to a prophage integration site is transferred

explanation.summary: In generalized transduction, during lytic growth random pieces of degraded host chromosome are accidentally packaged into phage capsids, so any host gene can be moved to a recipient, unlike specialized transduction which transfers only genes flanking the prophage.
explanation.whyCorrect: In generalized transduction, during lytic growth random pieces of degraded host chromosome are accidentally packaged into phage capsids, so any host gene can be moved to a recipient, unlike specialized transduction which transfers only genes flanking the prophage.

questions.stem: In this process a temperate phage integrates into the host chromosome and later excises imprecisely, carrying adjacent bacterial genes into new hosts. Why can only specific bacterial genes be transferred this way?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transfer requires direct cell-to-cell contact

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only DNA immediately flanking the prophage integration site is excised and packaged

choices.label: C
choices.text: Genes are taken up as naked DNA from the environment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only free plasmid genes are transferred

choices.label: E
choices.text: Any random host gene can be packaged

explanation.summary: In specialized transduction the prophage integrates at a specific chromosomal site; imprecise excision picks up only the immediately adjacent bacterial DNA, so just those flanking genes can be carried to the next host.
explanation.whyCorrect: In specialized transduction the prophage integrates at a specific chromosomal site; imprecise excision picks up only the immediately adjacent bacterial DNA, so just those flanking genes can be carried to the next host.
